# **Abstract** {.unnumbered}

### Objective

This study aimed to identify metabolic subgroups among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes based on glucose and hormonal responses from the oral glucose tolerance test and to quantify their associations with measures of body fat distribution.

### **Research Design and Methods**

K-means clustering was applied to 1,381 individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes from the ADDITION-PRO cohort. Clustering variables included measures of glucose, incretins (glucagon-like peptide-1 and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide), insulin sensitivity and glucagon suppression. Visceral (VAT) and subcutaneous (SAT) adipose tissue and body fat percentage were assessed by ultrasonography and bio-electrical impedance analysis. Associations between clusters and standardized measures of fat distribution were assessed through linear regression models adjusting for age, sex, smoking and physical activity.

### **Results**

Three clusters were identified. Cluster 3 had the highest glucose levels, lowest insulin sensitivity, and highest VAT, SAT, VAT/SAT ratio and body fat percentage (0.68, 0.28, 0.18 and 0.63 SD units higher than cluster 2, respectively). Despite similar glycemic profiles, cluster 1 had the lowest incretin secretion but highest glucagon suppression, while cluster 2 showed the opposite pattern. Cluster 1 had similar VAT to cluster 2 but higher SAT (0.25 SD), body fat percentage (0.15 SD units higher) and a lower VAT/SAT ratio (-0.23 SD).

### **Conclusions**

These findings suggest that incretin and glucagon responses capture metabolic heterogeneity beyond glycemic status and are associated with differences in body fat distribution. As findings are limited by the cross-sectional design, future studies should examine whether this heterogeneity predicts different risk trajectories and responses to prevention strategies.
